Guide October 15, 2025 • RecordWatchdog Team

The Ultimate Guide to Searching Public Records in California

A comprehensive, step-by-step guide to navigating California's vast public record systems, from business filings to court records and property deeds.

Advertisement

Google AdSense Unit

Understanding California's Public Record Landscape

California offers some of the most transparent and accessible public record systems in the United States, governed by the California Public Records Act (CPRA). Whether you are conducting due diligence on a potential business partner, researching property history, or looking up civil court cases, the state provides multiple robust online portals to access this information. However, because California is so large and decentralized, knowing exactly where to look can be challenging.

Step 1: Business Entity and Corporate Filings

The California Secretary of State's BizFile Online portal is the primary, official resource for searching Limited Liability Companies (LLCs), corporations, and limited partnerships registered in the state. When conducting a business entity search, you can query the database by the exact entity name, the name of the registered agent, or the specific entity number.

The search results will provide crucial due diligence information, including the entity's formation date, its current legal status (active, dissolved, suspended, or forfeited), the principal executive office address, and the name and address of the registered agent for service of process. Always verify that a business is in "Active" or "Good Standing" status before entering into any financial agreements.

Step 2: Navigating County Court Records

Unlike business filings, California's court system is highly decentralized. The state is divided into 58 counties, and each county's Superior Court maintains its own independent online database for civil, criminal, family law, and probate cases. There is no single, unified statewide database for all court records.

To search for a lawsuit or court case, you must first determine the correct county where the case was filed. This is typically the county where the defendant resides or where the incident in question occurred. Major counties like Los Angeles, San Diego, Orange, and Santa Clara offer robust, free online search tools. Smaller counties may require you to visit the courthouse in person or submit a formal written request to the court clerk.

Step 3: Property and Real Estate Records

Real estate records, including deeds, mortgages, liens, and property tax assessments, are maintained at the county level by the County Recorder or County Assessor's office. Most California counties now offer free online property search portals. By entering a property address or the owner's name, you can access the assessed value, tax payment history, and a chain of title showing previous owners.

Pro Tips for Effective and Accurate Searching

  • Use Exact Names: Always try to search using the exact legal name or entity number. Partial name searches (e.g., "Smith") can return thousands of results, making it nearly impossible to find the specific record you need.
  • Understand Sealed Records: Be aware that certain sensitive records, such as juvenile court cases, adoption records, and specific mental health proceedings, are legally sealed and are not available to the general public.
  • Beware of Third-Party Scams: Many commercial "people search" websites charge exorbitant fees for information that is available for free directly from official California .gov websites. Always prioritize official government portals.

The information provided in this article is for general informational and educational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Public record systems and laws vary by jurisdiction and are subject to change. Always verify information directly with the official government agency or consult with a qualified attorney.
